what_it_does: Defines the company UI-contract for the settings resource, mapping Claude Code's native settings.json scope hierarchy and merge rules onto the fabric session model, exposing only the fabric's live operating configuration slice and naming the gap where per-session settings files are not threaded.
observations: Settings have no fabric address or settings:// scheme; they are identified by scope and resolved from up to five layered files.
Scope precedence is managed policy > CLI flags > local .claude/settings.local.json > project .claude/settings.json > user ~/.claude/settings.json > built-in defaults.
Resolved values deep-merge: scalars override from higher scopes, arrays concatenate-and-dedupe, and fallbackModel is the one whole-chain exception.
The fabric supervisor spawns sessions with a fixed flag set and no --settings, so spawned sessions inherit only the service account's ~/.claude/settings.json.
The only company-readable configuration today is the fabric operating slice (cap, turn_timeout_s, permission, bind) via GET /health.
settings.get is the same GET /health wire read as fabric-config.get and permission.get, presented as a settings lens.
settings.act documents built spawn-time --settings and --add-dir levers while explicitly flagging per-session env override as residual and unverified.
Status is building; examples are synthetic and verification rows distinguish built, probe-verified, live-verified, and unverified states.
Settings keys and file paths are native Claude Code identifiers, not fabric addresses, and point to sibling resources like permission, model, auth, surfaces, diagnostics, and platform.
standouts: Honest naming of the per-session settings gap: the fabric spawn threads no settings file and only the operating slice is exposed.
settings.get, fabric-config.get, and permission.get all resolve to the same GET /health read with three retrieval lenses.
settings.act mixes built --settings/--add-dir support with a still-planned env override, each lever carrying explicit verification status.
Heavy use of contract schemas, op blocks, verification metadata, and synthetic/captured example markers.
conventions: Follows the contract-entry front-matter with type, resource, summary, schemes, status, and relates-to.
Uses contract:schema, contract:op, contract:example, and contract:error fenced blocks.
References sibling resources with [[...]] wiki-style links.
Includes verification metadata with state, run, date, and note.
Marks liveness as snapshot or none and examples as synthetic or captured.
Breaks from typical settings-editor contracts by refusing to expose per-scope settings.json contents and redirecting edits to operator filesystem acts.
concerns: settings.get overlaps fabric-config.get and permission.get, which the contract acknowledges but could confuse consumers.
settings.act mixes built, probe-verified, live-verified, and unverified levers in one operation, making status hard to read at a glance.
The planned per-session env override is repeatedly called out but still unwired, risking consumer expectation mismatch.
Examples for the built settings/add_dir levers remain synthetic because live verification is pending, so the built claim is not yet proven in real use.
notes: This file is the settings/config contract for the fabric: it tells consumers they can read the live operating slice via /health, that full per-scope settings editing is not a company operation, and that spawn-time --settings/--add-dir are built but env override remains planned.
questions: What is the exact JSON schema for the full settings.json key space beyond the linked docs?
How does the supervisor validate a --settings inline object or file before threading it?
What is the precise behavior when a managed policy key conflicts with a --settings value?
Which specific native keys hot-reload versus require a session restart?
What is the timeline or owner for wiring the residual per-session env override?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to contract the native config model with the per-session config gap named and surface the fabric's effective operating config as the one live read; the file does exactly that while also documenting planned/built spawn levers that are not yet fully live-verified.
apparent_themes: Honest capability boundaries: explicitly name what is not exposed or not wired.
Scope-precedence and merge-rule fidelity to the native Claude Code settings model.
Operator-controlled configuration: editable only via host filesystem or env vars plus restart, not company consumer ops.
Multi-lens retrieval: the same wire read exposed under settings, fabric-config, and permission resources.
Live-read preference: only the running fabric operating slice is company-readable.
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent a UI from building a fictional settings editor over the fabric by documenting the real native scope hierarchy, the fact that the supervisor spawn ignores settings files, and the exact spawn parameters that would need to be added to support per-session config.
novelty: The contract treats settings not as an editable resource but as a resolved, scope-merged, launch-time input, and deliberately exposes only a tiny operating-slice read while loudly naming the missing per-session config seam.
contribution: A precise, self-aware boundary description of what settings configuration the company fabric actually exposes and manipulates, plus a roadmap of native spawn-time levers (--settings, --add-dir, env) with honest verification status, so a fused system can avoid implementing a non-existent settings editor and route config questions to the correct lens or operator action.
summary_for_embedding: Company UI-contract entry for the settings resource, defining Claude Code's settings.json scope hierarchy, merge rules, the fabric's live operating-config read via GET /health, and the planned/built per-session spawn-time config levers.
intention_for_embedding: To contract the native Claude Code settings model against the company fabric's actual per-session config gap and expose only the operating configuration slice that is currently readable.
